title: Astralogy
content:
Astralogy - KalmarCTF

Astralogy - 216 Points
Pwn/Binary Exploitation
Current Date: 2/28/2026

Challenge Information:

No Hints

Thought process Walkthrough:

So after extracting the files, there are a couple of files.

challenge.iso hardening.patch run.sh

When running the run.sh, it brings me to a virtual machine with a hobby distro called astraOS. I have no idea what that is, so I’ll just search up some information.

There’s also one executable file called exploit, except ./exploit doesn’t work.

Before that, here is the text we get after we run run.sh:

-bash: cannot set terminal process group (31): Operation not permitted (EPERM)
-bash: no job control in this shell
astral@astral:~>

The main observations here being that the initial neofetch is being done as root. This makes me think that it MIGHT be a VM escape or kernel pwn. Now isn’t it just great. I can’t even do some basic ass ROP and I’m not even good at heap stuff. If I solve this, I’m genuinely gonna pop champagne. ok, I also notice it says that it cannot set terminal process group because the operation is not permitted. I googled it and terminal process groups are the ones that have control over the terminal. It says it failed meaning I have control over IO.

First thing I do is get some idea of the environment and which tools are available:

The ./exploit doesn’t work with execute format error.

For now, we exit the VM and check out the hardening.patch since that should be giving us a detailed review of what they did to harden the OS.

Main things here are:

wrmsr stands for write model specific register(wrmsr) and it writes some value to a register. The MSR_FMASK register is the syscall register which is the toggle for users to run CPU features. The hardening being done in this line is that the changed 0x200 to 0x600. In this case, the actual hexadecimal isn’t the key here, it’s the bits. These bits are CPU flags and it controls what the users can do. The binary for 0x200 is 0010 0000 0000. The 9th bit is flipped. That bit controls the interrupts on syscall, which prohibits users to interrupt while the kernel is still setting up the registers and everything. Because the whole thing here is that the user needs to syscall and use some CPU features and so the kernel needs to set up some stuf first and the 9th bit just prevents that from happening. By chaning the 0x200 to 0x600, it is changing the bits to 0110 0000 0000. Ok, so it’s also flipping the 10th bit, which as the comment suggests, is disabling DF or the Direction Flag. This only changes a String Instructions such as memcpy or memset. The direction flag changes the order it is being copied or just how the instruction is being carried out. Normally, it would go from front to back. The DF simiply chages that and does the instructions back to front. The end results are still the same and everything, but it’s hardened becaues nobody would know if your stuff is copying from front to back or the other way.

The next one here changes the above. The changes are in the - and +. Again, since it’s CPU, it’s just changing flags and whatnot. In the original one, only the 9th and 10th bit are set to 1. The comment above explains it well. Those two bits are:

Bit 9 SFXSR (Operating System Fast FXSAVE/FXRSTOR Support):
This bit is part of the two bits that make up SSE. The scond bit is the 10th bit which I will explain later on. This bit adds support for XMM0~XMM15 registers. Those registers are used for complex math such as for cryptography, graphics, or 3D rendering. Another common usage is when a process is being closed and the cpu must really quickly save the state and data it had on so that it can be opened later on in the same state. Basically, it just enables these registers for sum big math.

Bit 10 OSXMMEXCPT(Operating System XMM Unmasked Exception Support):
This is features for those XMM registers(0~15). This cpu flag is to ensure no problems when an error is made. Without this, if there was an errror happening in the cpu process, it’d just crash, but this flag gives the control back to the kernel and makes it able to shut it down safely. The whole thing is KIND OF in the name it being OS XMM register unamsked(not hidden error) support for exceptions.

The next part of the hardening is this:

title: Post Competition Thoughts
content:
Hmmmmmmm….

Ok so I’m actually writing this a couple of months after the competition and all I can say is that I didn’t walk into the competition very confident and I finished learning stuff.

I knew that KalmarCTF was going to be extremely hard and this is the competition I decided to do right after PicoCTF(which is a high school competition). I pretty much only joined to experience something extremely hard. Safe to say I absolutely experienced that.

I was also traveling abroad and didn’t have a lot of time to focus on the competition, so that’s another lousy excuse for you.

Point is, I learned some things about CPU flags and some specific registers that I didn’t previously know. Also, seeing the AstralOS also made me realize that hobby operating systems were possible. Nothing happened in this one, but I’ll still keep it around in this blog just to reminisce at and maybe eventually I’ll be good enough to actually get a placement in this CTF competition.

title: Motivation and Rough Planning
content:
Initial Remarks

Even though this is a bit off topic, this is pretty much my first actual post on this blog. I know the KalmarCTF one was before this but I made that thing a while ago so this is my first post made FOR this blog. It’s also the first project I’ll be fully documenting on this blog. Also, sorry if this post isn’t very structured. I’m still new to writing these blog posts. I’m sure I’ll get better at it as I make more posts.

Motivations

Alright guys, so I’ve known about LFS(Linux From Scratch) and BLFS(Beyong LFS) for quite a bit and since I’m also planning this summer to configure and move to Gentoo as my daily drive on my main laptop, I was just like hmmmmm, what if I also just made a fun LFS build just to learn a bit more about how linux works. Later on, I realized that I could make it a bit polished with BLFS and swaywm to it and rice it a little to make it a full hobby distro.

After brainstorming it a bit, I thought that it would be cool that since I’m interested in cybersecurity and specifically pwn and rev, to just make a minimal linux distro with some tools and stuff already made. I’m sure somebody else has already done this type of stuff, but it’s really just for my understanding of linux and also to have some fun with it.

That is literally the entire reason why I’m doing this lmaoooo💀💀. Maybe I’ll think of some more grand reason later on when I finish the distro and make a page for it.

Rough Plan

I already finished making the html pages for LFS and BLFS(holy shit it is long). As you can tell from the top, this post is made on May 24 and I really only plan on actually following the LFS and stuff in the Summer. I also got exams as well, so I’ll only be able to actually do stuff once school ends. From now till then, I’ll just try to read over the LFS to get a good idea of what it is doing.

Later, I’ll configure the LFS first by following the guide. As of now, I still don’t know jack about how it’s going to go so I can’t really say any specific thing but I’ll just follow the LFS to get a base CLI distro then I’ll follow the BLFS to get a swaywm thing on top. I honestly can NOT bother with GNOME or KDE stuff. I have a feeling that the apps and dependencies that come with those would kill me before I make a full distro.

After the initial configuration, I’ll make that into an iso to understand how that process works.

Then, I’ll go back and this time, I’ll diverge from the main LFS guide and install OPENRC as my init. Probably with some help from some AI and random geniuses in Discord groups I’m in.

Vision

So the vision is to obviously have a minimal distro with a minimal riced swaymd and a bunch of security features in the distro turned on. The init system will be OPENRC in the end and it’ll also come with some pwn and rev tools such as gef or ghidra or binary ninja.

Final Stuff

I really have no idea how big of a project I’m getting myself into but I’m pretty exicted to get started. Yuuhhhhh, in these projects, I’ll also try to give myself some listed homework at the bottom to just keep myself on track.

Homework

  • Skim through LFS and BLFS guide to get a rough idea
